title: PSA Density Calculator - Prostate Cancer Risk Assessment
tagline: Calculate PSA density (PSA level ÷ prostate volume) to improve prostate cancer screening accuracy beyond PSA alone.
intro: Enter your PSA level and prostate volume to calculate PSA density and assess prostate cancer risk.

error.invalid: Please enter valid positive values for both fields.
error.volumeZero: Prostate volume must be greater than zero.

about.heading: About the PSA density calculator
about.body:
Prostate-Specific Antigen (PSA) is a glycoprotein produced exclusively by prostate epithelial cells and secreted in large amounts into seminal fluid. A small fraction leaks into the bloodstream, where it can be measured by a simple blood test. PSA testing became widespread in the late 1980s and early 1990s as a screening tool for prostate cancer, and it dramatically increased the detection of early-stage disease. However, PSA testing has a fundamental limitation: PSA is prostate-specific, not cancer-specific. Any condition that irritates or enlarges the prostate — including benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH), prostatitis, urinary tract infections, and even vigorous bicycle riding or digital rectal examination — can elevate serum PSA without any cancer being present.

This lack of specificity is most problematic in men with moderately elevated PSA levels (roughly 4–10 ng/mL), where the positive predictive value for cancer is only about 25%. The majority of men in this range who undergo biopsy do not have prostate cancer, yet they experience the anxiety, discomfort, and rare but serious complications associated with the procedure. Finding ways to better stratify risk within this diagnostic grey zone is a major goal of prostate cancer research.

PSA density was introduced in the early 1990s as a simple refinement that accounts for the fact that larger prostates naturally produce more PSA. Benign prostatic hyperplasia, which is extremely common in older men, enlarges the prostate gland without cancer, thereby elevating total PSA in proportion to gland size. If a man has a PSA of 6 ng/mL but a very large prostate of 80 cc, much of that PSA elevation is likely benign. Another man with the same PSA of 6 ng/mL but a small prostate of 25 cc has a higher PSA density of 0.24 ng/mL/cc, which is more suspicious for cancer.

The formula is simply: PSA Density = PSA level (ng/mL) ÷ prostate volume (cc). Prostate volume is typically measured by transrectal ultrasound (TRUS) or MRI. Conventional TRUS uses an ellipsoid formula: volume = 0.52 × width × height × length. A density below 0.10 ng/mL/cc is reassuring and suggests the PSA elevation is more likely benign. Values between 0.10 and 0.15 ng/mL/cc are in a normal range, while 0.15–0.25 ng/mL/cc is considered elevated and warrants heightened surveillance or biopsy discussion. Values above 0.25 ng/mL/cc are high-risk and generally prompt biopsy.

In modern clinical practice, PSA density is particularly valuable when used alongside multiparametric MRI (mpMRI) findings. Studies such as the PRECISION trial demonstrated that mpMRI-targeted biopsy combined with PSA density thresholds reduces unnecessary biopsies while maintaining detection of clinically significant cancer. Many active-surveillance protocols also use PSA density to identify men whose low-risk cancers can be safely monitored rather than immediately treated.

Important limitations apply. PSA density depends on accurate prostate volume measurement, and TRUS measurements can vary by 10–20% between operators. MRI is more accurate but more expensive. Additionally, the PSA density threshold that best separates benign from malignant disease varies by PSA range, patient age, and race — African-American men tend to have smaller prostates and higher PSA density for a given PSA level. PSA density should always be interpreted in conjunction with clinical findings, digital rectal examination, and other biomarkers rather than as a standalone decision tool.

examples.heading: PSA density calculation examples
examples.intro: Clinical scenarios illustrating how the same PSA level carries different risk depending on prostate volume.

items.note: Low density despite elevated PSA — large BPH-related prostate likely accounts for most of the PSA rise.
Elevated density — small prostate with same PSA level is more suspicious and warrants further evaluation.
Elevated density (≥ 0.15 ng/mL/cc); biopsy discussion is appropriate, particularly if MRI is suspicious.
High density (> 0.25 ng/mL/cc) — high cancer probability; biopsy strongly recommended.

howto.heading: How to use the PSA density calculator
steps: Obtain a serum PSA result (ng/mL) from a recent blood test. Ensure no interfering activities (ejaculation, vigorous exercise, prostate massage) have occurred in the 48 hours prior to the test.
Obtain prostate volume in cubic centimetres (cc) from a transrectal ultrasound or MRI report. If not directly stated, volume = 0.52 × width × height × length from the imaging dimensions.
Enter the PSA level and prostate volume into the calculator fields.
Click Calculate. The PSA density and its risk category appear immediately.
Use the result as one input alongside PSA trend, digital rectal examination findings, and imaging when discussing biopsy or surveillance decisions with your urologist.

items.q: What is a normal PSA density?
items.a: A PSA density below 0.10 ng/mL/cc is generally reassuring and suggests the PSA elevation is more likely related to benign prostatic hyperplasia than to cancer. Values between 0.10 and 0.15 ng/mL/cc are borderline. Most guidelines consider 0.15 ng/mL/cc the threshold above which prostate biopsy should be more actively considered, though the decision always incorporates other clinical factors.

items.q: Why is PSA density better than PSA alone?
items.a: PSA alone does not account for the fact that larger prostates produce more PSA even without cancer. A man with a PSA of 6 ng/mL and a very large 80 cc prostate has a low PSA density of 0.075, consistent with BPH. Another man with the same PSA but a 25 cc prostate has a density of 0.24 — much more suspicious for cancer. PSA density therefore reduces false positives from BPH and improves the specificity of screening.

Prostate volume is most commonly measured by transrectal ultrasound (TRUS) using the prolate ellipsoid formula: volume = 0.52 × anteroposterior diameter × transverse diameter × longitudinal diameter. Multiparametric MRI provides more accurate volume measurements, particularly in glands with irregular shapes. The result is reported in cubic centimetres (cc) or millilitres (mL), which are equivalent.

items.q: Can PSA density replace prostate biopsy?
items.a: No. PSA density is a risk-stratification tool, not a definitive diagnostic test. It helps clinicians and patients weigh the probability of cancer against the risks of biopsy, but only tissue pathology can confirm or exclude prostate cancer. PSA density is best used in combination with other tools — mpMRI findings, PSA kinetics, and clinical examination — to make informed decisions about whether and when to biopsy.

items.q: Does PSA density apply to men on 5-alpha reductase inhibitors?
items.a: Men taking finasteride or dutasteride (5-alpha reductase inhibitors for BPH or hair loss) have their PSA levels reduced by approximately 50% after six months of treatment. When calculating PSA density in these patients, the measured PSA should be doubled to approximate the untreated PSA level before entering it into the calculator, or PSA density thresholds should be halved accordingly. Always inform your physician about any medications affecting PSA.

items.q: Is PSA density affected by age or race?
items.a: Yes. PSA levels and prostate volume both increase with age, but their relationship to cancer risk varies. Younger men (under 60) with elevated PSA density are at higher absolute risk than older men with the same density. African-American men have a higher prostate cancer risk at any given PSA density compared to white men, and some experts recommend lower biopsy thresholds in this population. PSA density should always be contextualised with age, family history, and ethnicity.
